segments.speaker: 讲话人29/30/31/32
segments.text: Listen to a talk on a history podcast. When you think of 19th century inventors, what comes to mind? Maybe, like, think of an inventor alone in his laboratory, tinkering with devices that no one else had ever dreamed of. Well, actually, there were often multiple people working on similar inventions at once, they improve on each other's work, compete or collaborate. Have you heard of Louis Latimer, who invented one kind of early air conditioner and improved the toilet systems in trains? Probably not. Latimer, as a black man, was excluded from many opportunities that his White peers enjoyed in the United States. He never even got to attend high school, but he taught himself, practiced on his own, to be a drafter-someone who makes technical drawings. Latimer's drawings were so skillful, they were practically works of art. He wrote poetry, and you can see his poetic sensibility even in his drafting work. After practicing to learn drafting, Latimer worked with several famous inventors over the span of his career. One was Hira Maxim. Working with Hira Maxim, Latimer developed and patented a major innovation in carbon filaments. A carbon filament is a thin wire inside a light bulb that glows with electricity to produce light. Latimer made these filaments stronger and longer lasting and easier to make. This was a major step in making lighting feasible and affordable for the general public. Module 2：
